comments.text: I made an attempt repository for exact values in the $p=2$ column of Problem #404: $f(a,2)\quad(1\le a\le256).$ Repository: https://github.com/KitaKen1/erdos-404-p2-landscape Visual table: https://kitaken1.github.io/erdos-404-p2-landscape/ In particular: $f(2,2)=254.$ This is worth singling out because the Problem #404 page records Lin's upper bound $f(2,2)\le254$; the repository gives the matching lower-bound witness and a two-sided Lean/lean4web check. Lean4web for $f(2,2)=254$: https://live.lean-lang.org/#url=https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2FKitaKen1%2Ferdos-404-p2-landscape%2Fmain%2Flean%2FS404_lean4web_2_2.lean Here $v_2(m)$ denotes the exponent of $2$ in $m$. The lower-bound side is just an explicit increasing list whose factorial sum is divisible by the claimed power of $2$. The upper-bound side is finite: by Legendre's formula,$v_2(n!)=\lfloor n/2\rfloor+\lfloor n/4\rfloor+\lfloor n/8\rfloor+\cdots,$ so to rule out exponent $K+1$, search modulo $2^{K+1}$, and once $v_2(n!)\ge K+1$, all later factorials are $0$ modulo $2^{K+1}$. For odd $a$, this immediately freezes the value at $f(a,2)=v_2(a!)$. For even $a$, cancellations make the values much wilder. The repository also records a few observations from the table. For example, the largest value found in $1\le a\le256$ is $f(34,2)=18444.$ AI usage: the repository, Lean files, and this comment were prepared with assistance from Codex 5.5 (xhigh reasoning), ChatGPT 5.5 Pro, and Claude Code (Fable 5).

I made an attempt repository for Problem #404's function $f(a,p)$, focusing on the odd-prime columns $p=3,5,7,11,13$: Repository: https://github.com/KitaKen1/erdos-404-odd-prime-landscape Visual table: https://kitaken1.github.io/erdos-404-odd-prime-landscape/ Contribution 1: witnesses for the special case $a=1$, i.e. lower bounds for $f(1,p)$. The displayed certificates give $f(1,3)\ge 20000$, $f(1,5)\ge 20000$, $f(1,7)\ge 20000$, $f(1,11)\ge 5000$, and $f(1,13)\ge 5000$. These are finite witnesses, not claims of infinitude; each witness is an explicit increasing list, checked directly modulo $p^K$. On the GitHub Pages visual table, one can click an entry and inspect the concrete list $a_1,\ldots,a_n$. Contribution 2: rows for the remaining starts $a>1$, i.e. data for $f(a,p)$ beyond the $a=1$ case. Contribution 2-1: exact rows for $a>1$. These mean the search finds a witness at exponent $K$ and rules out exponent $K+1$. Some rows currently displayed are $f(3,3)=3$, $f(39,3)=25$, $f(700,3)=363$, $f(110,5)=30$, $f(236,5)=61$, $f(42,7)=7$, $f(113,11)=11$, and $f(163,13)=13$. Small Lean4Web examples: $f(3,3)=3$: Lean4Web; $f(4,7)=0$: Lean4Web; $f(6,7)=0$: Lean4Web. Contribution 2-2: lower-bound rows for $a>1$. Some cells are marked as scan-survivors. For those cells I am not claiming an exact value. The displayed lower bound is only the baseline certificate from the one-term list $(a)$: it proves $f(a,p)\ge v_p(a!)$. Examples include $f(40,3)\ge18$, $f(300,5)\ge74$, $f(294,7)\ge48$, $f(297,11)\ge29$, and $f(299,13)\ge24$. Whether a larger exponent is possible is left open. This is in the direction of the discussion around Problem #404, especially Terence Tao's comments about $f(1,5)$ : https://www.erdosproblems.com/forum/thread/404 AI disclosure: the search programs, organization, Lean files, and this comment were prepared with assistance from Codex, ChatGPT, and Claude Code (Fable 5).

It seems to me that $f(1,5)$ is already extremely large, and possibly even infinite; for each $m$, it appears that there are lots of ways to recursively find a set of factorials up to $(5m-1)!$ and starting at $1!$ that sum to something that divides at least as many powers of $5$ as $5!$. For instance $$ 1! + 4! = 100_5$$ $$1!+4!+10!+11! = 42121430000_5$$ $$1! + 4! + 10! + 11! + 20! + 21! + 23! = 102401442411110300413412001000000_5$$ Things get a little tricky every time one reaches $5m$ is a multiple of $5^2$ or higher powers, but there is a combinatorial explosion of possible cases and it seems very likely that one could continue this sort of process for quite a while.

I do not understand the first two questions. The first one makes no sense: the uniform bound on $f(a,p)$ is not possible, since we can find $k$ as large as we want if we allow $a$ to be sufficiently large. Taking a look into the Graham-Erdos problem book (p. 79), I also see that it is not what they wanted: the power of $p$ in $p^k$ and the number of summands in $a_1!+\cdots+a_k!$ were not supposed to be the same. To make changes minimal I suggest to replace the first half of the problem (i.e., the first two questions) with: For which positive integers $a$ and which primes $p$ there exists a finite least upper bound $f(a,p)$ on the exponents $m$ such that\[ p^m | (a_1!+\cdots+a_k!) \]for some $k\geq1$ and some integers $a=a_1<\cdots< a_k$? The comment on Lin's result should then be $f(2,2)=254$. The third question seems to be ok. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Corrected, thanks - sorry about the typo!

Thanks for the correction. I haven't seen that they asked about the behavior of $f(a,p)$, but I suppose that would be interesting to resolve as well. :-) The word "growth" might be a bit misleading as, for instance,\[ f(1,2)=0,\ f(2,2)=254,\ f(3,2)=1,\ f(4,2)=6,\ f(5,2)=3,\ldots, \]but clearly $\lim_{a\to\infty} f(a,p)=\infty$, so I suppose that's ok to say too.
